expectations.text: The 'description:' field in the SKILL.md frontmatter contains an explicit exclusion clause using the literal phrase 'NOT for' (capitalized exactly that way) followed by at least one excluded use case — not just a description of what the skill does.
expectations.evidence: The description field reads: 'Validates JSON files against JSON Schema specifications (drafts 7, 2019-09, 2020-12). Use when asked to validate JSON, check schema compliance, or verify data files against a schema. Supports glob patterns, auto-discovers schemas, and reports detailed violations with paths and expected vs actual values.' The phrase 'NOT for' does not appear anywhere in the description or anywhere else in the SKILL.md.

expectations.text: The SKILL.md body contains at least one explicit anti-pattern block with three labeled subheadings drawn from {Symptom, Problem, Solution} OR {What it looks like, Why it's wrong, What to do instead} — each labeled and on its own line/bold heading, naming a specific JSON-validation pitfall (e.g., schema drift, silent parse errors, validating untrusted input, missing required fields).
expectations.evidence: No anti-pattern block exists in the SKILL.md body. The document contains a Usage section, an Implementation section (Steps 1–8), a Supported JSON Schema Keywords table, and an Error Handling table. The Error Handling table lists situations and behaviors but is not structured with Symptom/Problem/Solution or What it looks like/Why it's wrong/What to do instead labeled subheadings.

expectations.text: The SKILL.md contains BOTH a positive use list AND a separate negative list (e.g., a 'When to Use' section with '✅ Use for:' and '❌ NOT for:' bullets, OR distinct 'When to Use' and 'When NOT to Use' sections) — not merely a single 'Use this for X' paragraph.
expectations.evidence: The SKILL.md has no 'When to Use' or 'When NOT to Use' section, no ✅/❌ bullet lists, and no dual positive/negative structure. The document opens with a one-paragraph description in the frontmatter and then moves directly to Usage (command syntax + examples) and Implementation steps.

claims.claim: The skill includes YAML frontmatter with name, description, and triggers
claims.evidence: Confirmed: frontmatter contains name: json-validator, description: (one sentence), and triggers: list with 5 entries.
Steps 1–8 are present: runtime detection, file discovery, schema resolution, validator installation, validation scripts, result collection, structured output, and exit codes.

claims.claim: Full keyword coverage table for JSON Schema drafts 7, 2019-09, 2020-12
claims.evidence: The Supported JSON Schema Keywords table exists but does not differentiate by draft version — it is a single unified table covering common keywords without indicating which draft introduced them or applies per draft.

claims.claim: Error handling table for common failure cases (missing files, parse errors, missing schema, network refs, permissions)
claims.evidence: Error Handling table contains exactly these cases: File not found, Invalid JSON, Schema not found, Invalid schema, Network $ref, Permission denied.

suggestions.reason: All three assertions test for skill-foundry structural conventions (exclusion clauses, anti-pattern blocks, dual use/not-use lists) that a naive skill author would omit entirely — which is exactly what happened here. This is a strong signal the assertions are genuinely discriminating and not trivially satisfied. No changes needed on discriminability.

suggestions.assertion: The SKILL.md body contains at least one explicit anti-pattern block...
suggestions.reason: The assertion specifies two valid label sets ({Symptom, Problem, Solution} OR {What it looks like, Why it's wrong, What to do instead}) but doesn't mention other plausible structural variants a skill author might use (e.g., 'Pitfall / Root cause / Fix'). If the intent is strict, the assertion is clear; if partial credit for close-but-different structures is intended, the assertion should enumerate or prohibit those alternatives explicitly.

eval_feedback.overall: Assertions are well-targeted: all three check structural conventions that only appear in skills written with the skill-foundry framework in mind. A without-skill run correctly fails all three. No false-confidence risk.
